title: Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Median income or consumption per day vs. GDP per capita
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps the relationship between national economic output and the typical daily resources available to individuals across countries in 2024. The horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $50,000, while the vertical axis shows median income or consumption per day from $2 to over $50. Circle size corresponds to population, with the largest markers representing India and China. A strong positive correlation is visible, where higher GDP per capita coincides with higher median daily income or consumption. The United States sits at the top right, with GDP per capita exceeding $50,000 and median daily resources above $50. Japan and European nations cluster in the upper middle range, with GDP between $20,000 and $50,000 and daily medians between $20 and $50. China and Turkey occupy the middle of the distribution, with GDP around $20,000 and daily medians near $15 to $20. India, Indonesia, and Vietnam form a group with GDP between $5,000 and $10,000 and daily medians between $5 and $10. At the lower end, countries including Burundi, DR Congo, and Madagascar show GDP below $2,000 and daily medians under $5. The data points for African nations generally sit below the main trend line formed by Asian and European countries at similar GDP levels. The visual pattern shows that as GDP per capita increases, median income or consumption per day rises in a consistent upward trajectory across the global sample.
